The Bieber (Completed 05/07/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 2
Speaker 1: All right, guys. Well, welcome to Themeplate 2, The Bieber. How can we not have it, of
course, created by two Canadians. I'm not saying we are Bieber lovers. We did make a
Radiohead reference just before, so don't question our musical tastes too early in the
game. But what can we borrow from Bieber when it comes to writing email copy?
Turns out, not a lot, but we are going to take the name "sorry." Sorry is a very powerful
word. Think about some examples of this argument between partners. Then finally,
someone says "Sorry," and then the other says, "That's all I needed you to say. That's all
I needed to hear." All of a sudden, all this fighting finally ends. They put those
differences and pride aside and become resourceful.
An apology goes a long way. It creates that resourcefulness and renewed optimism
we're looking for, and it absolves the other person of any feelings or doubts that they
were the ones at fault. That's what we want to activate here. We want to clear the past
so that our prospect can get more present and excited about where we're taking them.
As a side note when you apologize on behalf of other things, people, paradigm,
oppressors, competitors, in your industry, it subtly positions you a trusted and
benevolent authority. You need to be a pretty high up authority to apologize on behalf
of someone else. What if like ... it's like how parents will apologize on behalf of their
kids. That puts them at that position of authority, and it's the same thing when you
apologize on behalf of your industry or competitors who just couldn't know better,
because they're not at that same level as you or as benevolent as you.
That's what we're going for here. That's the vibe. This is the Themeplate. We're going to
start off with the apology, just flat out. That gets attention. What is this person sorry
for? Then we'll get into that moment of highest tension. We're going to get into the
ways they've tried to solve it. We'll show that it wasn't their fault. Then we'll offer that
way out, that better way, that more resourceful way that comes at the other end of an
apology. Then we'll have that call to action. We'll qualify them in that exclusively
empowered way.
We're really using a lot of the same elements we've already written about and explained
throughout this module. We're just using a different type of container. This is going to
be useful as either a second email to send if they didn't opt in from that first exclusively
empowered call-out. This will just give them that release, that release from blame in the
past and that resourcefulness that this time it can work. That's what we are aiming for
here.
Let's just go through an example, and this is also from our 10X Launches launch. You
may have seen this. You may not have. This is something that you could send to people
when they didn't sign up for that first email. If you signed up from the first call-out, you
probably wouldn't have gotten this one. Let's get into it.
Subject line, sorry. That is a hell of an open loop, one word, five letters, but just one
massive, open loop. What is this person apologizing for? I'm sorry, Ryan. I'm sorry that
the online course you just paid $5K for didn't warn you about how hard it would be to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 2 --

The Bieber (Completed 05/07/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 2
execute all those tiny email icons on the funnel map, that the success of your funnel,
and heck, your entire business, fully hinges on writing those little things and writing
them fast, that an expertly curated concoction of binaural beats, bullet proof coffee,
and brain drugs with bad names haven't been enough to help you blast through it.
I'm sorry that despite your best efforts to do it yourself, you've been struck down by
confusing, overcomplicated, and impractical systems written by old, mostly dead
copywriters who didn't have to contend with unceasing slack notifications, impossible
deadlines, and more impossible screaming six-year-olds. This is, once again, all that stuff
that's part of that moment of highest tension and being super specific, down to bullet
proof coffee, and brain drugs, and slack notifications, and screaming six-year-olds, these
are all real things that are prospect and may be you as someone who is in this course
experiences day by day. It's fully grounded in that reality.
I'm really, really sorry. On behalf of all these things that have put you in this perilous
place with the success of your launch dangling in the balance, please accept my special
invitation to make it up to you. This is where we're framing our call to action as that
apology, as that making up for all these things that didn't work or that just haven't been
serving them. That's another way to frame your call to action.
Starting next week, Joe and I will be hosting the launch and profit workshops. This is a
must attend if you're ... and this is where we're going to get into those exclusively
empowered points which you saw before a party gets started next Tuesday. Click here
to stay within blast radius for when the copy bombs starting falling, and then our sign-
off. Then like you saw before, we could just bring in a little more curiosity and sell the
event more and with another call to action on don't forget to grab your seat.
But the main elements here are really quite simply apologizing on behalf of your
industry, apologizing on behalf of their situations and just those oppressors. By
apologizing, you're really positioning yourself as the authority, the authority who's
capable of apologizing of behalf of something and offering a better alternative.
That is The Bieber. Sorry for using something called The Bieber. Sorry, not sorry. I'm
going to stop saying sorry now, because it's the most Canadian thing ever. That is it.
Use this Themeplate if you did not get the opt-in or the click with Themeplate number
one. This is a really great followup for that. That's it. We got another one coming up, so I
will see you in the next walkthrough.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
